elements.sent-2411-001: Anticipating the multimodality of future events lays the foundation for safe autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, multimodal motion prediction for traffic agents has been clouded by the lack of multimodal ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existing works predominantly adopt the winner-take-all training strategy to tackle this challenge, yet still suffer from limited trajectory diversity and misaligned mode confidence.
elements.sent-2411-004: While some approaches address these limitations by generating excessive trajectory candidates, they necessitate a post-processing stage to identify the most representative modes, a process lacking universal principles and compromising trajectory accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-005: We are thus motivated to introduce ModeSeq, a new multimodal prediction paradigm that models modes as sequences.
elements.sent-2411-006: Unlike the common practice of decoding multiple plausible trajectories in one shot, ModeSeq requires motion decoders to infer the next mode step by step, thereby more explicitly capturing the correlation between modes and significantly enhancing the ability to reason about multimodality.
elements.sent-2411-007: Leveraging the inductive bias of sequential mode prediction, we also propose the Early-Match-Take-All (EMTA) training strategy to diversify the trajectories further.
elements.sent-2411-008: Without relying on dense mode prediction or rule-based trajectory selection, ModeSeq considerably improves the diversity of multimodal output while attaining satisfactory trajectory accuracy, resulting in balanced performance on motion prediction benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Moreover, ModeSeq naturally emerges with the capability of mode extrapolation, which supports forecasting more behavior modes when the future is highly uncertain.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{parallel_mode.pdf} \caption{Parallel mode modeling} \label{fig:parallel_mode} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{sequential_mode.pdf} \caption{Sequential mode modeling} \label{fig:sequential_mode} \end{subfigure} \caption{A comparison between parallel and sequential mode modeling. While parallel mode modeling (\cref{fig:parallel_mode}) decodes multimodal trajectories in one shot, our sequential mode modeling (\cref{fig:sequential_mode}) reasons about multiple plausible futures step by step, which captures the relationships between modes to avoid producing indistinguishable trajectories and confidence scores.} \label{fig:comparison}
elements.sent-2411-010: Handling the intricate uncertainty presented in the real world is one of the major hurdles in autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-011: One aspect of the uncertainty lies in the multimodal behavior of traffic agents, \ie, multiple instantiations of an agent's future may be compatible with a given observation of the past.
elements.sent-2411-012: Without characterizing the multimodal distribution of agent motions, autonomous vehicles may fail to interact with the surroundings in a safe and human-like manner.
elements.sent-2411-013: For this reason, advanced decision-making systems demand a motion predictor to forecast several plausible and representative trajectories of critical agents~\cite{ding2021epsilon, li2023marc .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Although multimodality has long been the central topic studied in motion prediction, this problem has not been fundamentally solved owing to the unavailability of multimodal ground truth, \ie, only one possibility is observable in real-world driving data.
elements.sent-2411-015: To struggle with this dilemma, most existing works adopt the winner-take-all (WTA) strategy~\cite{lee2016stochastic for training~\cite{cui2019multimodal, chai2019multipath, liang2020learning, zhou2022hivt, varadarajan2022multipath++, nayakanti2022wayformer, zhou2023query, shi2022mtr .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Under this strategy, only the best among all predicted trajectories will receive supervision signals for regression, while all the remaining will be masked in the training loss.
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite being the current standard practice in the research community, the WTA solution has been found to cause mode collapse easily and produce indistinguishable trajectories~\cite{makansi2019overcoming, rupprecht2017learning, thiede2019analyzing , further confusing the learning of mode scoring~\cite{lin2024eda .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: As a remedy, some recent research intends to cover the ground-truth mode by generating a massive number of trajectory candidates~\cite{varadarajan2022multipath++, nayakanti2022wayformer, shi2022mtr , from which the most representative ones are heuristically selected based on post-processing methods such as non-maximum suppression (NMS).}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, such a post-processing step requires carefully tuning the hyperparameters, \eg, the thresholds in NMS.
elements.sent-2411-020: Even if the hyperparameters were well-tuned, they might not fit various scenarios with diverse road conditions, leading to inferior generalization.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, performing dense mode prediction followed by rule-based post-processing may significantly sacrifice trajectory accuracy in practice~\cite{shi2022mtr , given that correctly extracting the best trajectories from a large set of candidates is non-trivial.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The limitations of mainstream methods prompt us to seek an end-to-end solution that directly produces a sparse set of diverse, high-quality, and representative agent trajectories, eliminating the need for dense mode prediction and heuristic mode selection.
elements.sent-2411-023: To begin with, we identify a commonality of existing multimodal motion predictors, that all trajectory modes are decoded in one shot, which we dub parallel mode modeling as depicted in \cref{fig:parallel_mode .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Despite its efficiency, this paradigm neglects the relationship between the predicted modes, hindering models from decoding diverse multimodal output.
elements.sent-2411-025: For anchor-free methods with parallel mode modeling~\cite{ngiam2021scene, varadarajan2022multipath++, zhou2022hivt, nayakanti2022wayformer, zhou2023query , the distinction between the decoded trajectories depends entirely on the difference in sub-network parameters, which cannot be guaranteed under the unstable WTA training.}
elements.sent-2411-026: For this reason, some of these solutions turn to dense mode prediction and rely on post-processing steps to diversify the output~\cite{varadarajan2022multipath++, nayakanti2022wayformer .}
elements.sent-2411-027: While anchor-based approaches offload the duties of ensuring diversity onto the input anchors~\cite{phan2020covernet, chai2019multipath, zhao2020tnt, shi2022mtr , determining a sparse set of anchors that can adapt to specific scenarios is challenging, compelling all these approaches to employ excessive anchors for dense mode prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Under the paradigm of parallel mode modeling, producing multimodal trajectories with sparse mode prediction faces significant obstacles.
elements.sent-2411-029: To tackle these challenges, this paper explores sequential mode modeling (ModeSeq), a completely different pathway toward sparse multimodal motion prediction.
elements.sent-2411-030: As illustrated in \cref{fig:sequential_mode , we attempt to construct a chain of modes when decoding the future from the scene embeddings, producing only one plausible trajectory and the corresponding confidence at each decoding step.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Compared with parallel prediction, our approach puts more effort into capturing the correlation between modes, asking the model to tell what the next mode should be and how much confidence it has conditioned on the mode embeddings at previous steps.
elements.sent-2411-032: By giving the model a chance to look at the prior modes and learning the factorized joint latent space of multiple futures, we tremendously boost the capability of reasoning about multimodality and characterizing the full distribution without the reliance on dense mode prediction, post-processing tricks, and all manner of anchors.
elements.sent-2411-033: To strengthen the capacity of models under our new paradigm, we develop an iterative refinement framework similar to DETR-like decoders~\cite{carion2020end, zhou2023query, shi2022mtr, zhou2024smartrefine , which is powered by reordering the mode embeddings in between decoding layers.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, leveraging the order of modes in the sequence, we further propose the Early-Match-Take-All (EMTA) training strategy, which can encourage the decoder to match the ground truth as early as possible.
elements.sent-2411-035: Meanwhile, our EMTA scheme also enforces the decoder to vacate the duplicated modes to cover the missing futures at the cost of negligible degradation in trajectory accuracy, thereby achieving better mode coverage and easing the learning of confidence scoring.
elements.sent-2411-036: We validate ModeSeq on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset~\cite{ettinger2021large and the Argoverse 2 Motion Forecasting Dataset~\cite{Argoverse2 , where we achieve more balanced performance in terms of mode coverage, mode scoring, and trajectory accuracy, compared with representative motion forecasting methods such as QCNet~\cite{zhou2023query and MTR~\cite{shi2022mtr .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Furthermore, our approach naturally emerges with the capability of mode extrapolation thanks to sequential mode modeling, which enables predicting a dynamic number of modes on demand.
elements.sent-2411-038: Multimodality has been a dark cloud in the field of motion prediction.
elements.sent-2411-039: Early works employ generative models to sample multimodal trajectories~\cite{tang2019multiple, rhinehart2019precog, lee2017desire, gupta2018social, hong2019rules , but they are susceptible to mode collapse.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Modern motion predictors~\cite{cui2019multimodal, chai2019multipath, liang2020learning, zhou2022hivt, varadarajan2022multipath++, nayakanti2022wayformer, zhou2023query, shi2022mtr mostly follow the paradigm of multiple choice learning~\cite{lee2016stochastic , where multiple trajectory modes are produced directly from mixture density networks~\cite{bishop1994mixture .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Due to the lack of multimodal ground truth, these methods adopt the WTA training strategy~\cite{lee2016stochastic , which is unstable and fails to deal with mode collapse fundamentally~\cite{makansi2019overcoming, rupprecht2017learning, thiede2019analyzing .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: To mitigate this issue, a line of research performs dense mode prediction, \ie, decoding excessive trajectory candidates for better mode coverage~\cite{varadarajan2022multipath++, nayakanti2022wayformer, shi2022mtr .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Among these works, some equip the decoder with anchors~\cite{shi2022mtr, zhao2020tnt, chai2019multipath, phan2020covernet to achieve more stable training.}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, dense mode prediction necessitates the rule-based selection of the most representative trajectories from a large set of candidates, risking the precision of trajectories and the generalization ability across a wide range of scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-045: This paper provides new insights into multimodal problems by introducing the paradigm of sequential mode modeling and the EMTA training strategy, pursuing an end-to-end solution that produces a sparse set of diverse, high-quality, and representative trajectories directly.
elements.sent-2411-046: Sequential modeling has found many applications in motion prediction and traffic modeling.
elements.sent-2411-047: On the one hand, applying sequential modeling to the time dimension results in trajectory encoders and decoders based on recurrent networks~\cite{hochreiter1997long, cho2014learning, alahi2016social, deo2018convolutional, rhinehart2019precog, rhinehart2018r2p2, mercat2020multi, lee2017desire, gupta2018social, salzmann2020trajectron++, varadarajan2022multipath++, tang2019multiple or Transformers~\cite{vaswani2017attention, ngiam2021scene, liu2021multimodal, giuliari2021transformer, yu2020spatio, nayakanti2022wayformer, zhou2022hivt, zhou2023query, seff2023motionlm, zhou2024behaviorgpt, philion2024trajeglish , which can facilitate the learning of temporal dynamics.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: In particular, recent advances in motion generation~\cite{seff2023motionlm, zhou2024behaviorgpt, philion2024trajeglish have shown that factorizing the joint distribution of multi-agent time-series in a social autoregressive manner~\cite{alahi2016social, tang2019multiple, rhinehart2019precog can better characterize the evolution of traffic scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: On the other hand, some works utilize sequential modeling in the agent dimension for multi-agent motion prediction~\cite{sun2022m2i, rowe2023fjmp .}
elements.sent-2411-050: For example, M2I~\cite{sun2022m2i uses heuristic methods to label influencers and reactors from pairs of agents, followed by predicting the marginal distribution of the influencers and the conditional distribution of the reactors.}
elements.sent-2411-051: FJMP~\cite{rowe2023fjmp extends M2I to model the joint distribution of an arbitrary number of agents, where the joint future trajectories of agents are factorized using a directed acyclic graph.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Our work is the first attempt that employs sequential prediction in the mode dimension, which enhances the understanding of multimodal behavior by capturing the correlation between modes.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{modeseq.pdf} \caption{Overview of the ModeSeq framework. Left: We stack multiple ModeSeq layers with mode rearrangement in between to iteratively refine the multimodal output under the Early-Match-Take-All (EMTA) training strategy. Right: Each ModeSeq layer consists of a Memory Transformer module for capturing mode-wise dependencies and a Context Transformer module for retrieving the scene embeddings produced by the encoder, operating in a recurrent fashion to decode a sequence of trajectory modes.} \label{fig:modeseq}
elements.sent-2411-053: Denote $S$ as the input of motion prediction models, which encompasses the map elements represented as $M$ polygonal instances and the $T$-step historical trajectories of $A$ traffic agents (\eg, vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists) in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-054: The models are tasked with forecasting $K$ plausible trajectory modes per agent of interest, each comprising $\hat{T}$ waypoints and an associated confidence score.
elements.sent-2411-055: These trajectories are desired to be representative, reflecting distinct behavior modes of agents and properly measuring the likelihood of each mode via the estimated confidence.
elements.sent-2411-056: Typical motion predictors employ the encoder-decoder architecture, where an encoder computes the embedding $\Psi$ from the scene input, based on which a decoder learns the embeddings $\{\bm{m _{i,k}\}_{k \in \{1,\ldots,K\}}$ of the $i$-th agent's future modes.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Without loss of generality, the following simplifies $\bm{m _{i,k}$ as $\bm{m _k$ to discuss the prediction for a single agent, which can be extended for multiple agents by repeating the same decoding process.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Given $\bm{m _k$, a prediction head then outputs a trajectory $\hat{\bm{y _k = [\hat{\bm{y _k^1,\ldots,\hat{\bm{y _k^{\hat{T ]$ and a confidence score $\hat{\bm{\phi _k$ via simple modules such as multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs).}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The whole pipeline can be summarized as \begin{equation}\begin{dcases} \Psi = \encoder\left(S\right) \,, \{\bm{m}_k\}_{k \in \{1,\ldots,K\}} = \decoder\left(\Psi\right) \,, \hat{\bm{y}}_k, \hat{\bm{\phi}}_k = \head\left(\bm{m}_k\right), \quad k \in \{1,\ldots,K\} \,. \end{dcases} \end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-060: Prior works formulate multimodal decoding as a problem of set prediction~\cite{carion2020end, vinyals2015order .}
elements.sent-2411-061: For instance, most cutting-edge methods employ DETR-like decoders~\cite{carion2020end to produce the joint embeddings of multiple modes from learnable or anchor-based queries that are permutation-equivariant~\cite{varadarajan2022multipath++, nayakanti2022wayformer, zhou2023query, shi2022mtr .}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Ideally, the joint mode embeddings should be supervised by the ground-truth multimodal distribution, akin to the application of set prediction in object detectors~\cite{carion2020end, liu2022dab, meng2021conditional, zhu2020deformable where each object query receives supervision signals via optimal bipartite matching.}
elements.sent-2411-063: However, real-world driving data contains only one instantiation of scene evolution, which compels motion prediction solutions to adopt the winner-take-all (WTA) matching~\cite{lee2016stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-064: As a result, only the mode embeddings of the best-predicted trajectories get optimized, which will easily degenerate the models into learning multiple mode embeddings independently.
elements.sent-2411-065: The inability to jointly optimize all modes explains why DETR-like motion decoders fail to avoid duplicated trajectories without the use of non-maximum suppression (NMS), given that gathering multiple predicted trajectories around the most probable regions is expected to achieve lower training loss when only one ground-truth future is presented during training.
elements.sent-2411-066: To facilitate reasoning about multimodality in the absence of multimodal ground truth, our ModeSeq framework requires the decoder to conduct chain-based factorization on the joint embeddings, which is demonstrated as follows: \begin{equation}\bm{m}_{t} = \decoder\left(\Psi,\, \{\bm{m}_k\}_{k \in \{1,\ldots,t-1\}}\right),\, t \in \{1,\ldots,K\}\,. \label{eq:seq}\end{equation} With such a factorization that converts the unordered set of modes into a sequence, the correlation between modes can be naturally strengthened, as the mode to be decoded depends on the ones that appear previously.
elements.sent-2411-067: Further equipping this framework with appropriate model implementation and training scheme has the potential to offer better mode coverage and scoring without severe sacrifice in trajectory accuracy, which we introduce in the following sections.
elements.sent-2411-068: Since this work focuses on the decoding of multimodal trajectories, we simply adopt QCNet~\cite{zhou2023query as the scene encoder, which is one of the de facto best practices in industry and academia due to its symmetric modeling in space and time leveraging relative positional embeddings~\cite{shaw2018self .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: This encoder, on the one hand, exploits a hierarchical map encoding module based on map-map self-attention to produce the map embedding of shape $[M, D]$, with $D$ referring to the hidden size.
elements.sent-2411-070: On the other hand, the encoder consists of Transformer modules that factorize the space and time axes, including temporal self-attention, agent-map cross-attention, and agent-agent self-attention.
elements.sent-2411-071: These three types of attention are grouped and interleaved twice to yield the agent embedding of shape $[A, T, D]$, which constitutes the final scene embeddings together with the map embedding.
elements.sent-2411-072: In principle, any scene encoding method can fit into our ModeSeq framework with reasonable efforts.
elements.sent-2411-073: This section illustrates the detailed structure of a single ModeSeq layer, which consists of a Memory Transformer module and a Context Transformer module.
elements.sent-2411-074: Stacking multiple ModeSeq layers can further improve the performance via iterative refinement, which will be discussed in \cref{sec:multi .. Routine.}
elements.sent-2411-075: We introduce the decoding procedure of the $\ell$-th ModeSeq layer, with the right-hand side of \cref{fig:modeseq depicting the first layer (\ie, $\ell=1$).}
elements.sent-2411-076: The layer is designed to recurrently output a sequence of mode embeddings $[\bm{m _1^{(\ell) ,\ldots,\bm{m _K^{(\ell) ]$, where we slightly complicate the notation of modes with a superscript that identifies the layer index.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The input of the layer includes the scene embedding $\Psi$ yielded by the encoder and the mode embeddings produced by the $(\ell-1)$-th decoding layer, \ie, $[\bm{m _1^{(\ell-1) ,\ldots,\bm{m _K^{(\ell-1) ]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Since the latter does not apply to the first layer, we introduce an embedding $\bm{e \in \mathbb{R ^D$ to serve as the output of the ``$0$-th layer'', which is randomly initialized at the beginning of training.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The same learnable $\bm{e}$ is shared across the $K$ input embeddings of the first layer: \begin{equation}\bm{m}_k^{(0)} = \bm{e},\, k \in \{1,\ldots,K\}\,. \end{equation} Before starting the decoding, we create an empty sequence $\Omega_0^{(\ell) = [\,]$ with the subscript and superscript indicating the $0$-th decoding step and the $\ell$-th layer, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This sequence will be used to keep track of the mode embeddings produced at various steps.
elements.sent-2411-081: At the $t$-th decoding step, we employ a Memory Transformer module and a Context Transformer module to update $\bm{m _{t ^{(\ell-1)}$ to become $\bm{m _{t ^{(\ell)}$, leveraging the information in the memory bank $\Omega_{t-1 ^{(\ell)}$ and the scene embedding $\Psi$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The output mode embedding $\bm{m _{t ^{(\ell)}$ is then pushed to the end of the sequence $\Omega_{t-1 ^{(\ell)}$ to obtain $\Omega_{t ^{(\ell) = [\bm{m _1^{(\ell) ,\ldots,\bm{m _{t ^{(\ell) ]$, which will serve as the input at the $(t+1)$-th decoding step.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: After going through $K$ decoding steps, we use a prediction head to transform each of the mode embeddings stored in $\Omega_K^{(\ell)}$ into a specific trajectory and a corresponding confidence score via MLPs.
elements.sent-2411-084: The following paragraphs detail the modules constituting a ModeSeq layer and discuss the differences between our approach and other alternatives.. Memory Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-085: The Memory Transformer takes charge of modeling the sequential dependencies of trajectory modes.
elements.sent-2411-086: At the $t$-th decoding step of the $\ell$-th layer, this module takes as input $\Omega_{t-1 ^{(\ell)}$ and $\bm{m _t^{(\ell-1)}$, the memory bank for the current layer and the $t$-th mode embedding derived from the last layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Since we desire the generation of the $t$-th mode embedding $\bm{m _t^{(\ell)}$ to be aware of the existence of the preceding modes, we treat $\bm{m _t^{(\ell-1)}$ as the query of the Transformer module, which retrieves the memory bank in a cross-attention manner: \begin{equation}\hat{\bm{m}}_t^{(\ell)} = \memory\left(\text{query}{=}\bm{m}_t^{(\ell-1)},\,\text{key}/\text{value}{=}\Omega_{t-1}^{(\ell)}\right)\,.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: In this way, the information in $\Omega_{t-1 ^{(\ell)}$ is assimilated into $\bm{m _t^{(\ell-1)}$ to produce $\hat{\bm{m _t^{(\ell)}$, which is a query feature conditioned on the modes up to the $(t-1)$-th decoding step.. Context Transformer.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: To derive scene-compliant modes, we must provide the query feature with a specific scene context.
elements.sent-2411-090: To this end, we use the Context Transformer module to refine the conditional query $\hat{\bm{m _t^{(\ell)}$ with the scene embeddings output by the encoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Specifically, the $t$-th mode embedding $\bm{m _t^{(\ell)}$ is computed by enriching $\hat{\bm{m _t^{(\ell)}$ with $\Psi$ using cross-attention: \begin{equation}\bm{m}_t^{(\ell)} = \context\left(\text{query}=\hat{\bm{m}}_t^{(\ell)},\,\text{key}/\text{value}=\Psi\right)\,.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Considering the high complexity of performing global attention, we decompose the Context Transformer into three separate modules in practice, including mode-time cross-attention, mode-map cross-attention, and mode-agent cross-attention, each of which takes as input only a subset of the embeddings contained in $\Psi$.
elements.sent-2411-093: First, the mode-time cross-attention fuses the query feature with the historical encoding belonging to the agent of interest, enabling the query to adapt to the specific agent.
elements.sent-2411-094: Second, we aggregate the map information surrounding the agent of interest into the query feature leveraging the mode-map cross-attention, which contributes to the map compliance of the forecasting results.
elements.sent-2411-095: Finally, utilizing the mode-agent cross-attention module to fuse the neighboring agents' latest embeddings promotes the model's social awareness.
elements.sent-2411-096: After going through these three modules, the conditional query $\hat{\bm{m _t^{(\ell)}$ eventually becomes $\bm{m _t^{(\ell)}$, which is now context-aware.. Prediction Head.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Given the conditional, context-aware mode embedding $\bm{m _t^{(\ell)}$, we use an MLP head to output the $t$-th trajectory $\hat{\bm{y _t^{(\ell)}$ and another to estimate the corresponding confidence score $\hat{\bm{\phi _t^{(\ell)}$: \begin{equation}\begin{dcases} \hat{\bm{y}}_t^{(\ell)} = \mlp\left(\bm{m}_t^{(\ell)}\right)\,, \hat{\bm{\phi}}_t^{(\ell)} = \mlp\left(\bm{m}_t^{(\ell)}\right)\,. \end{dcases} \end{equation} Comparison with DETR-Like Decoders.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: In contrast to motion decoders~\cite{varadarajan2022multipath++, nayakanti2022wayformer, zhou2023query, shi2022mtr inspired by DETR~\cite{carion2020end , where the relationships between modes are completely neglected~\cite{varadarajan2022multipath++, nayakanti2022wayformer or weakly modeled by mode-mode self-attention~\cite{zhou2023query, shi2022mtr , modeling modes as a sequence strengthens mode-wise relational reasoning thanks to the conditional dependence in generating multimodal embeddings, which is beneficial to eliminating duplicated trajectories.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Furthermore, it is worth noting that DETR-like approaches can only decode a fixed number of modes, as the number of learnable/static anchors cannot be changed once specified at the start of training.
elements.sent-2411-100: By contrast, our ModeSeq framework supports decoding more/less modes at test time, which can be simply achieved by changing the number of decoding steps.
elements.sent-2411-101: This characteristic can be helpful since the degree of uncertainty varies by scenario.
elements.sent-2411-103: The ModeSeq layer can be viewed as a sort of recurrent network~\cite{hochreiter1997long, cho2014learning due to its parameter sharing across all decoding steps, though its core components are modernized with Transformers to achieve impressive performance.}
elements.sent-2411-104: While typical recurrent networks compress the memory into a single hidden state, which is often lossy, the Memory Transformer inside a ModeSeq layer allows for direct access to all prior mode embeddings, naturally scaling the capacity of the memory as the number of modes grows.
elements.sent-2411-105: Single-layer mode sequences may have limited capability of learning high-quality mode representations.
elements.sent-2411-106: In particular, if the layer happens to produce unrealistic or less likely modes at the first few decoding steps, the learning of the later modes may be unexpectedly disturbed.
elements.sent-2411-107: Inspired by DETR~\cite{carion2020end , we develop an iterative refinement framework by stacking multiple ModeSeq layers and applying training losses to the output of each layer.}
elements.sent-2411-108: As shown in the left part of \cref{fig:modeseq , all layers except for the first one take as input the mode embeddings output from the last round of decoding, refining the features with the scene context.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Crucially, we introduce the operation of mode rearrangement in between layers, which corrects the order of the embeddings in the mode sequence to encourage decoding trajectory modes with monotonically decreasing confidence scores.. Mode Rearrangement.
elements.sent-2411-110: Before transitioning from the $\ell$-th to the $(\ell+1)$-th ModeSeq layer, we sort the mode embeddings stored in the memory bank $\Omega_K^{(\ell)}$ according to the descending order of the confidence scores predicted from them.
elements.sent-2411-111: The sorted mode embeddings will then be sequentially input to the $(\ell+1)$-th ModeSeq layer for recurrent decoding.
elements.sent-2411-112: Through iterative refinement with mode rearrangement, the trajectories and the order of modes become more scene-compliant and more reasonable, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-113: The WTA training strategy~\cite{lee2016stochastic is blamed for producing overlapped trajectories and indistinguishable confidence scores~\cite{lin2024eda, makansi2019overcoming, rupprecht2017learning, thiede2019analyzing .}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Fortunately, our approach has the opportunity to opt for a more advanced training method thanks to the paradigm of sequential mode modeling.
elements.sent-2411-115: In this section, we propose the EMTA loss, which leverages the order of modes to define the positive and negative samples toward better mode coverage and confidence scoring without significantly sacrificing trajectory accuracy.

elements.sent-2411-116: Typical WTA loss optimizes only the trajectory with the minimum displacement error with respect to the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-117: In comparison, our EMTA loss optimizes the matched trajectory decoded at the earliest recurrent step.
elements.sent-2411-118: For example, if both the second and the third trajectories match the ground truth, only the second one will be optimized, regardless of which one has the minimum error.
elements.sent-2411-119: To this end, we search over the $K$ predictions to acquire the collection of mode indices associated with matched trajectories: \begin{equation}G^{(\ell)} = \left\{k \mid k \in\left\{1,\cdots,K\right\} \wedge \mathds{1}\left\{\match\left(\hat{\bm{y}}_k^{(\ell)},\, \bm{y}\right)\right\} \right\}\,,\end{equation} where $G^{(\ell)}$ denotes the set of qualified mode indices in the $\ell$-th ModeSeq layer, $\mathds{1}\{\cdot\}$ represents the indicator function, and $\match(\cdot,\,\cdot)$ defines the criterion for a match given the ground-truth trajectory $\bm{y}$.
elements.sent-2411-120: The implementation of $\match(\cdot,\,\cdot)$ can be flexible, depending on the trajectory accuracy demanded by practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-121: For instance, on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset~\cite{ettinger2021large , we decide whether a predicted trajectory is a match based on the velocity-aware distance thresholds defined in the Miss Rate metric of the benchmark; while on the Argoverse 2 Motion Forecasting Dataset~\cite{Argoverse2 , a matched trajectory is expected to have less than $2$-meter final displacement error.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Given $G^{(\ell)}$, we determine the unique positive sample's index $\hat{k ^{(\ell)}$ as follows, with all the remaining modes treated as negative samples: \begin{equation}\hat{k}^{(\ell)} = \begin{dcases} \min_{k \in G^{(\ell)}} k \text{if } |G^{(\ell)}| > 0 \,; \operatorname*{argmin}_{k} \distance\left(\hat{\bm{y}}_k^{(\ell)},\, \bm{y}\right) \text{otherwise} \,, \end{dcases} \end{equation} where $|\cdot|$ denotes the cardinality of a set, and $\distance(\cdot,\,\cdot)$ measures the average displacement error between trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-123: This strategy for label assignment encourages the model to decode matched trajectories as early as possible by treating the earliest instead of the best matches as positive samples.

elements.sent-2411-182: This paper introduces ModeSeq, a modeling framework that achieves sparse multimodal motion prediction via sequential mode modeling.
elements.sent-2411-183: The framework comprises a mechanism of sequential multimodal decoding, an architecture of iterative refinement with mode rearrangement, and a training strategy of Early-Match-Take-All label assignment.
elements.sent-2411-184: As an alternative to the unordered multimodal decoding and the winner-take-all training strategy, ModeSeq achieves state-of-the-art results on motion prediction benchmarks and exhibits the characteristic of mode extrapolation, creating a new path to solving multimodal problems.. Limitations.
